revision.text:
{{神社 |名称=新城神社 |画像=[[File:新城天主堂 xincheng catholic church - panoramio.jpg|250px]] |所在地=[[花蓮港庁]]花蓮郡研海庄大字新城（現在の[[花蓮県]][[新城郷]]博愛路64号） |祭神=[[能久親王]]
[[大国魂命]]
[[大己貴命]]
[[少彦名命]] |社格=社 |創建=[[1937年]]（[[昭和]]12年） | 緯度度 = 24 | 緯度分 = 7| 緯度秒 = 46.63 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N 　 | 経度度 = 121|経度分 = 38| 経度秒 = 59.17| E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | ISO = TW |地図 = Taiwan |ラベル位置 = left }} {{tw monument |name=新城神社舊址 |formername= |image= |imagename= |othername= |formalname= |level=県定古蹟 |class=[[牌坊]] |date=2002年9月23日 |time= |location={{flagicon|ROC}}[[花蓮県]][[新城鄉]]新城村博愛路64號 |website= |reg_url =https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 }} '''新城社'''（しんじょうしゃ）あるいは'''新城神社'''（しんじょうじんじゃ）は、[[日本統治時代の台湾]]東部[[花蓮港庁]]{{仮リンク|花蓮郡|zh|花蓮郡}}{{仮リンク|研海庄|zh|研海庄}}に存在した神社である。日本兵と台湾原住民の抗争事件の跡地に建立された慰霊碑「新城事件記念石」を基に、昭和戦前期に建立された{{r|文}}{{r|金子}}。第二次世界大戦後、神社址の敷地は[[カトリック教会]]に買い取られ、「新城天主堂」が建造された。だが[[鳥居]]、基座などはそのまま残っている{{r|文}}。神社の遺跡は2005年2月23日、[[花蓮県]]当局によって県の古蹟に指定された{{r|文}}。 == 沿革 == 日本統治時代初頭の[[1896年]]（[[明治]]29年） 台湾東部において[[日本陸軍]]と[[台湾原住民]][[タロコ族]]の衝突事件・[[新城事件]]が発生し、日本兵10数名が殺害された。事件現場の当地には犠牲になった日本兵の慰霊碑「殉難將士瘞骨碑」（1920年建立）、「弔魂碑」（1915年建立）、「陸軍監視哨殉難記念碑」（1920年建立）等が建立された{{r|潘}}が、後の昭和12年（1937年）10月25日、「新城神社」が建立された{{r|金子}}{{r|潘}}。鎮座当日は[[花蓮港庁]]長・藤村寬太や[[花蓮郡]]守・吉田治久らが参列し、青年による相撲、舞踏表演、剣道などの実演が挙行された{{r|金子}}。 第二次世界大戦後、[[台湾国民党]]政府は兵士を派遣して神社の建物を破壊したところ、何人もの兵士が発狂したという。結果、敷地は無人のまま放置された{{r|潘}}。だが民国45年（[[1956年]]）スイス国籍の[[神父]]・傅光業が神社の敷地の西側に幼稚園を建設し、1959年にはスイス国籍の神父・沙智勇が神社の東側に神父会院を建立した{{r|潘}}。さらに民国53年（1964年），オスティーネ聖教会は{{仮リンク|天主教花蓮教区|zh|天主教花蓮教区}}名義で神社の敷地を買い上げ、天主堂{{r|潘}}を建立した。天主堂は民国55年（1966年）に落成した{{r|潘}}。 == 神社遺跡 == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|第三鳥居]] 新城神社には三基の鳥居があった。第一鳥居は現在の新城公園傍ら、新城天主堂への参道に存在し、1979年に新城郷公所は鳥居の上部の両端を切断した上「新城公園」の四字をはめ込んだ[[牌坊]]に改造した{{r|潘}}。第二鳥居は協会の入口にあり、上部には「天主教會」四字がある{{r|潘}}。第二、第三鳥居は両側に新たな支柱が設けられた。これは新城天主堂の戴宏基神父による、増築である{{r|潘}}。 手水鉢は天主堂の内部に移設され「[[洗礼]]池」に使用されている{{r|潘}}。 == ギャラリー == File:新城神社舊址.jpg|神社本殿基座の址に建立された聖母マリア像 File:新城事件殉難將士瘞骨碑.jpg|「殉難將士瘞骨碑」（[[新城事件]]記念石） File:新城社第一鳥居.jpg|第一鳥居 == 参考 == *[[新城事件]] *[[理蕃政策]] == 参考文献 == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=新城神社舊址 |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=文化部文化資產局 |work=國家文化資產資料庫 |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=金子展也；陳嫺若 譯 |title=《遠渡來台的日本諸神》|publisher=野人文化 |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=潘繼道 |title=〈新城事件歷史現場變遷〉 |journal=《臺灣學通訊》 |volume=82 |publisher=國立臺灣圖書館 |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28－29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:しんしようしんしや}} [[Category:台湾の神社]] [[Category:花蓮県の歴史]] [[Category: 現存しない台湾の建築物]]

monument |name=新城神社舊址 |formername= |image= |imagename= |othername= |formalname= |level=県定古蹟 |class=[[牌坊]] |date=2002年9月23日 |time= |location={{flagicon|ROC}}[[花蓮県]][[新城郷]]新城村博愛路64号 |website= |reg_url '''新城社'''（しんじょうしゃ）あるいは'''新城神社'''（しんじょうじんじゃ）は、[[日本統治時代の台湾]]東部[[花蓮港庁]]{{仮リンク|花蓮郡|zh|花蓮郡}}{{仮リンク|研海庄|zh|研海庄}}に存在した神社である。日本兵と台湾原住民の抗争事件の現場に建立された慰霊碑「新城事件記念石」を基に、昭和戦前期に建立された{{r|文}}{{r|金子}}。第二次世界大戦後、神社址の敷地は[[カトリック教会]]に買い取られ、「新城天主堂」が建造された。だが[[鳥居]]、基座などはそのまま残っている{{r|文}}。神社の遺跡は2005年2月23日、[[花蓮県]]当局によって県の古蹟に指定された{{r|文}}。 == 沿革 == 日本統治時代初頭の[[1896年]]（[[明治]]29年） 台湾東部において[[日本陸軍]]と[[台湾原住民]][[タロコ族]]の衝突事件・[[新城事件]]が発生し、日本兵10数名が殺害された。事件現場の当地には犠牲になった日本兵の慰霊碑「殉難將士瘞骨碑」（1920年建立）、「弔魂碑」（1915年建立）、「陸軍監視哨殉難記念碑」（1920年建立）等が建立された{{r|潘}}が、後の昭和12年（1937年）10月25日、「新城神社」が建立された{{r|金子}}{{r|潘}}。鎮座当日は[[花蓮港庁]]長・藤村寬太や[[花蓮郡]]守・吉田治久らが参列し、青年による相撲、舞踏表演、剣道などの実演が挙行された{{r|金子}}。 第二次世界大戦後、[[中国国民党|国民党]]政府は兵士を派遣して神社の建物を破壊したところ、何人もの兵士が発狂したという。結果、敷地は無人のまま放置された{{r|潘}}。だが民国45年（[[1956年]]）スイス国籍の[[神父]]・傅光業が神社の敷地の西側に幼稚園を建設し、1959年にはスイス国籍の神父・沙智勇が神社の東側に神父会院を建立した{{r|潘}}。さらに民国53年（1964年），オスティーネ聖教会は{{仮リンク|天主教花蓮教区|zh|天主教花蓮教区}}名義で神社の敷地を買い上げ、天主堂{{r|潘}}を建立した。天主堂は民国55年（1966年）に落成した{{r|潘}}。 == 神社遺跡 == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|第三鳥居]] 新城神社には三基の鳥居があった。一の鳥居は現在の新城公園傍ら、新城天主堂への参道に存在し、1979年に新城郷公所は鳥居の上部の両端を切断した上「新城公園」の四字をはめ込んだ[[牌坊]]に改造した{{r|潘}}。二の鳥居は協会の入口にあり、上部には「天主教會」四字がある{{r|潘}}。二と三の鳥居は両側に新たな支柱が設けられた。これは新城天主堂の戴宏基神父による、増築である{{r|潘}}。 手水鉢は天主堂の内部に移設され「[[洗礼]]池」に使用されている{{r|潘}}。 == ギャラリー == File:新城神社舊址.jpg|神社本殿基座の址に建立された聖母マリア像 File:新城事件殉難將士瘞骨碑.jpg|「殉難將士瘞骨碑」（[[新城事件]]記念石） File:新城社第一鳥居.jpg|一の鳥居 == 参考 == *[[新城事件]] *[[理蕃政策]] == 参考文献 == [[Sukuna-bikona-no-Mikoto]] |Rank=Shrine |Founded=[[1937]] (Shōwa 12) |Latitude degrees = 24 |Latitude minutes = 7 |Latitude seconds = 46.63 |N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 121 |Longitude minutes = 38 |Longitude seconds = 59.17 |E/W = E |ISO = TW |Map = Taiwan |Label position = left }} {{tw monument |name=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |formername= |image= |imagename= |othername= |formalname= |level=County Historic Site |class=[[Paifang]] |date=September 23, 2002 |time= |location={{flagicon|ROC}}[[Hualien County]] [[Xincheng Township]] Shinjō Village Boai Road 64 |website= |reg_url =https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 }} '''Shinjō Shrine''' (しんじょうしゃ) or '''Shinjō Jinja''' (しんじょうじんじゃ) was a shrine located in eastern Taiwan’s [[Hualien Port Prefecture]] {{ill|Hualien District|ja|花蓮郡}} {{ill|Kenkai Village|ja|研海莊}} during the Japanese colonial period. The shrine was built in the Shōwa era before World War II on the site of a commemorative stone “Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone” established in remembrance of Japanese soldiers who fell during conflicts with Taiwan’s indigenous peoples{{r|Bun}}{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, the shrine grounds were sold to the [[Catholic Church]], where the "Shinjō Catholic Church" was built. However, remnants such as the torii gates and foundations remain intact{{r|Bun}}. On February 23, 2005, the former shrine site was designated as a county historic site by the [[Hualien County]] authorities{{r|Bun}}. == History == At the beginning of the Japanese colonial era in [[1896]] (Meiji 29), a conflict known as the [[Shinjō Incident]] occurred in eastern Taiwan between the [[Imperial Japanese Army]] and the indigenous [[Taroko people]], resulting in the deaths of more than 10 Japanese soldiers. Memorial monuments such as the “Monument for the Fallen Soldiers” (established in 1920), the “Condolence Monument” (established in 1915), and the “Army Observation Post Fallen Commemoration Monument” (established in 1920) were built on the site{{r|Pan}}. Later, on October 25, 1937 (Shōwa 12), the "Shinjō Shrine" was constructed{{r|Kaneko}}{{r|Pan}}. At the shrine’s consecration ceremony, dignitaries such as the Governor of [[Hualien Port Prefecture]], Fujimura Kanta, and the Hualien District Governor, Yoshida Haruhisa, attended. Celebrations included youth sumo, dance performances, and kendo demonstrations{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, soldiers sent by the [[Kuomintang]] government destroyed the shrine buildings, reportedly causing several soldiers to suffer mental breakdowns. The site was subsequently left abandoned{{r|Pan}}. However, in 1956, Swiss priest Father Fu Guangye built a kindergarten on the western part of the shrine’s grounds, and in 1959, Swiss priest Father Sha Zhiyong constructed a clergy residence on the eastern side{{r|Pan}}. In 1964, the Ostine Catholic Church purchased the shrine grounds under the name {{ill|Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja|天主教花蓮教区}}, and a Catholic church was established{{r|Pan}}. The church was completed in 1966{{r|Pan}}. == Shrine Remains == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|The third torii]] Shinjō Shrine had three torii gates. The first torii stands near Shinjō Park along the pathway to the Catholic church and was converted into a [[paifang]] in 1979, with the words "Shinjō Park" embedded after cutting off the upper ends of the gate{{r|Pan}}. The second torii, located at the entrance to the church, bears the inscription “Catholic Church”{{r|Pan}}. Both the second and third torii have been reinforced with new supporting columns as part of renovations by the church’s priest, Dai Hongji{{r|Pan}}. The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a [[baptismal font]]{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya; translated by Chen Xianruo |title=The Japanese Gods Who Crossed to Taiwan |publisher=Wild Man Culture |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=Pan Jidao |title=Changes in the Historical Site of the Shinjō Incident |journal=Taiwan Studies Bulletin |volume=82 |publisher=National Taiwan Library |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28–29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinjō Shrine}} [[Category:Shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Taiwan]] [[ja:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E8%8A%B1%E8%93%AE%E6%B8%AF%E5%BA%81)]]

[[Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Sukuna-bikona-no-Mikoto]] |Rank=Shrine |Founded=[[1937]] (Shōwa 12) |Latitude degrees = 24 |Latitude minutes = 7 |Latitude seconds = 46.63 |N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 121 |Longitude minutes = 38 |Longitude seconds = 59.17 |E/W = E |ISO = TW |Map = Taiwan |Label position = left }} {{tw monument |name=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |formername= |image= |imagename= |othername= |formalname= |level=County Historic Site |class=[[Paifang]] |date=September 23, 2002 |time= |location={{flagicon|ROC}}[[Hualien County]] [[Xincheng Township]] Shinjō Village Boai Road 64 |website= |reg_url =https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 }} '''Shinjō Shrine''' (しんじょうしゃ) or '''Shinjō Jinja''' (しんじょうじんじゃ) was a shrine located in eastern Taiwan’s [[Hualien Port Prefecture]] {{ill|Hualien District|ja|花蓮郡}} {{ill|Kenkai Village|ja|研海莊}} during the Japanese colonial period. The shrine was built in the Shōwa era before World War II on the site of a commemorative stone “Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone” established in remembrance of Japanese soldiers who fell during conflicts with Taiwan’s indigenous peoples{{r|Bun}}{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, the shrine grounds were sold to the [[Catholic Church]], where the "Shinjō Catholic Church" was built. However, remnants such as the torii gates and foundations remain intact{{r|Bun}}. On February 23, 2005, the former shrine site was designated as a county historic site by the [[Hualien County]] authorities{{r|Bun}}. == History == At the beginning of the Japanese colonial era in [[1896]] (Meiji 29), a conflict known as the [[Shinjō Incident]] occurred in eastern Taiwan between the [[Imperial Japanese Army]] and the indigenous [[Taroko people]], resulting in the deaths of more than 10 Japanese soldiers. Memorial monuments such as the “Monument for the Fallen Soldiers” (established in 1920), the “Condolence Monument” (established in 1915), and the “Army Observation Post Fallen Commemoration Monument” (established in 1920) were built on the site{{r|Pan}}. Later, on October 25, 1937 (Shōwa 12), the "Shinjō Shrine" was constructed{{r|Kaneko}}{{r|Pan}}. At the shrine’s consecration ceremony, dignitaries such as the Governor of [[Hualien Port Prefecture]], Fujimura Kanta, and the Hualien District Governor, Yoshida Haruhisa, attended. Celebrations included youth sumo, dance performances, and kendo demonstrations{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, soldiers sent by the [[Kuomintang]] government destroyed the shrine buildings, reportedly causing several soldiers to suffer mental breakdowns. The site was subsequently left abandoned{{r|Pan}}. However, in 1956, Swiss priest Father Fu Guangye built a kindergarten on the western part of the shrine’s grounds, and in 1959, Swiss priest Father Sha Zhiyong constructed a clergy residence on the eastern side{{r|Pan}}. In 1964, the Ostine Catholic Church purchased the shrine grounds under the name {{ill|Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja|天主教花蓮教区}}, and a Catholic church was established{{r|Pan}}. The church was completed in 1966{{r|Pan}}. == Shrine Remains == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|The third torii]] Shinjō Shrine had three torii gates. The first torii stands near Shinjō Park along the pathway to the Catholic church and was converted into a [[paifang]] in 1979, with the words "Shinjō Park" embedded after cutting off the upper ends of the gate{{r|Pan}}. The second torii, located at the entrance to the church, bears the inscription “Catholic Church”{{r|Pan}}. Both the second and third torii have been reinforced with new supporting columns as part of renovations by the church’s priest, Dai Hongji{{r|Pan}}. The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a [[baptismal font]]{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya; translated by Chen Xianruo |title=The Japanese Gods Who Crossed to Taiwan |publisher=Wild Man Culture |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=Pan Jidao |title=Changes in the Historical Site of the Shinjō Incident |journal=Taiwan Studies Bulletin |volume=82 |publisher=National Taiwan Library |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28–29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinjō Shrine}} [[Category:Shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Taiwan]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[ja:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E8%8A%B1%E8%93%AE%E6%B8%AF%E5%BA%81)]]

[[Sukuna-bikona-no-Mikoto]] |Rank=Shrine |consecration_year=[[1937]] (Shōwa 12) |Latitude degrees = 24 |Latitude minutes = 7 |Latitude seconds = 46.63 |N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 121 |Longitude minutes = 38 |Longitude seconds = 59.17 |E/W = E |ISO = TW |Map = Taiwan |Label position = left }} {{tw monument |name=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |formername= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |imagename= |othername= |formalname= |level=County Historic Site |class=[[Paifang]] |date=September 23, 2002 |time= |location={{flagicon|ROC}}[[Hualien County]] [[Xincheng Township]] Shinjō Village Boai Road 64 |website= |reg_url =https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 }} '''Shinjō Shrine''' (しんじょうしゃ) or '''Shinjō Jinja''' (しんじょうじんじゃ) was a shrine located in eastern Taiwan’s [[Hualien Port Prefecture]] {{ill|Hualien District|ja|花蓮郡}} {{ill|Kenkai Village|ja|研海莊}} during the Japanese colonial period. The shrine was built in the Shōwa era before World War II on the site of a commemorative stone “Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone” established in remembrance of Japanese soldiers who fell during conflicts with Taiwan’s indigenous peoples{{r|Bun}}{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, the shrine grounds were sold to the [[Catholic Church]], where the "Shinjō Catholic Church" was built. However, remnants such as the torii gates and foundations remain intact{{r|Bun}}. On February 23, 2005, the former shrine site was designated as a county historic site by the [[Hualien County]] authorities{{r|Bun}}. == History == At the beginning of the Japanese colonial era in [[1896]] (Meiji 29), a conflict known as the [[Shinjō Incident]] occurred in eastern Taiwan between the [[Imperial Japanese Army]] and the indigenous [[Taroko people]], resulting in the deaths of more than 10 Japanese soldiers. Memorial monuments such as the “Monument for the Fallen Soldiers” (established in 1920), the “Condolence Monument” (established in 1915), and the “Army Observation Post Fallen Commemoration Monument” (established in 1920) were built on the site{{r|Pan}}. Later, on October 25, 1937 (Shōwa 12), the "Shinjō Shrine" was constructed{{r|Kaneko}}{{r|Pan}}. At the shrine’s consecration ceremony, dignitaries such as the Governor of [[Hualien Port Prefecture]], Fujimura Kanta, and the Hualien District Governor, Yoshida Haruhisa, attended. Celebrations included youth sumo, dance performances, and kendo demonstrations{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, soldiers sent by the [[Kuomintang]] government destroyed the shrine buildings, reportedly causing several soldiers to suffer mental breakdowns. The site was subsequently left abandoned{{r|Pan}}. However, in 1956, Swiss priest Father Fu Guangye built a kindergarten on the western part of the shrine’s grounds, and in 1959, Swiss priest Father Sha Zhiyong constructed a clergy residence on the eastern side{{r|Pan}}. In 1964, the Ostine Catholic Church purchased the shrine grounds under the name {{ill|Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja|天主教花蓮教区}}, and a Catholic church was established{{r|Pan}}. The church was completed in 1966{{r|Pan}}. == Shrine Remains == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|The third torii]] Shinjō Shrine had three torii gates. The first torii stands near Shinjō Park along the pathway to the Catholic church and was converted into a [[paifang]] in 1979, with the words "Shinjō Park" embedded after cutting off the upper ends of the gate{{r|Pan}}. The second torii, located at the entrance to the church, bears the inscription “Catholic Church”{{r|Pan}}. Both the second and third torii have been reinforced with new supporting columns as part of renovations by the church’s priest, Dai Hongji{{r|Pan}}. The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a [[baptismal font]]{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya; translated by Chen Xianruo |title=The Japanese Gods Who Crossed to Taiwan |publisher=Wild Man Culture |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=Pan Jidao |title=Changes in the Historical Site of the Shinjō Incident |journal=Taiwan Studies Bulletin |volume=82 |publisher=National Taiwan Library |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28–29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinjō Shrine}} [[Category:Shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Taiwan]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[ja:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E8%8A%B1%E8%93%AE%E6%B8%AF%E5%BA%81)]]

[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[ja:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE_(%E8%8A%B1%E8%93%AE%E6%B8%AF%E5%BA%81)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]][[zh:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Shrine |name=Shinjō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Shinjou_Tenshudou_xincheng_catholic_church_-_panoramio.jpg|250px]] |location=[[Hualien Port Prefecture]] Hualien District Kenkai Village Shinjō (current [[Hualien County]] [[Xincheng Township]] Boai Road 64) |Deities=[[Prince Nogi]]
[[Ōkunitama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Sukuna-bikona-no-Mikoto]] |Rank=Shrine |consecration_year=[[1937]] (Shōwa 12) |Latitude degrees = 24 |Latitude minutes = 7 |Latitude seconds = 46.63 |N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 121 |Longitude minutes = 38 |Longitude seconds = 59.17 |E/W = E |ISO = TW |Map = Taiwan |Label position = left }} {{tw monument |name=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |formername= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |imagename= |othername= |formalname= |level=County Historic Site |class=[[Paifang]] |date=September 23, 2002 |time= |location={{flagicon|ROC}}[[Hualien County]] [[Xincheng Township]] Shinjō Village Boai Road 64 |website= |reg_url =https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 }} '''Shinjō Shrine''' (しんじょうしゃ) or '''Shinjō Jinja''' (しんじょうじんじゃ) was a shrine located in eastern Taiwan’s [[Hualien Port Prefecture]] {{ill|Hualien District|ja|花蓮郡}} {{ill|Kenkai Village|ja|研海莊}} during the Japanese colonial period. The shrine was built in the Shōwa era before World War II on the site of a commemorative stone “Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone” established in remembrance of Japanese soldiers who fell during conflicts with Taiwan’s indigenous peoples{{r|Bun}}{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, the shrine grounds were sold to the [[Catholic Church]], where the "Shinjō Catholic Church" was built. However, remnants such as the torii gates and foundations remain intact{{r|Bun}}. On February 23, 2005, the former shrine site was designated as a county historic site by the [[Hualien County]] authorities{{r|Bun}}. == History == At the beginning of the Japanese colonial era in [[1896]] (Meiji 29), a conflict known as the [[Shinjō Incident]] occurred in eastern Taiwan between the [[Imperial Japanese Army]] and the indigenous [[Taroko people]], resulting in the deaths of more than 10 Japanese soldiers. Memorial monuments such as the “Monument for the Fallen Soldiers” (established in 1920), the “Condolence Monument” (established in 1915), and the “Army Observation Post Fallen Commemoration Monument” (established in 1920) were built on the site{{r|Pan}}. Later, on October 25, 1937 (Shōwa 12), the "Shinjō Shrine" was constructed{{r|Kaneko}}{{r|Pan}}. At the shrine’s consecration ceremony, dignitaries such as the Governor of [[Hualien Port Prefecture]], Fujimura Kanta, and the Hualien District Governor, Yoshida Haruhisa, attended. Celebrations included youth sumo, dance performances, and kendo demonstrations{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, soldiers sent by the [[Kuomintang]] government destroyed the shrine buildings, reportedly causing several soldiers to suffer mental breakdowns. The site was subsequently left abandoned{{r|Pan}}. However, in 1956, Swiss priest Father Fu Guangye built a kindergarten on the western part of the shrine’s grounds, and in 1959, Swiss priest Father Sha Zhiyong constructed a clergy residence on the eastern side{{r|Pan}}. In 1964, the Ostine Catholic Church purchased the shrine grounds under the name {{ill|Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja|天主教花蓮教区}}, and a Catholic church was established{{r|Pan}}. The church was completed in 1966{{r|Pan}}. == Shrine Remains == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|The third torii]] Shinjō Shrine had three torii gates. The first torii stands near Shinjō Park along the pathway to the Catholic church and was converted into a [[paifang]] in 1979, with the words "Shinjō Park" embedded after cutting off the upper ends of the gate{{r|Pan}}. The second torii, located at the entrance to the church, bears the inscription “Catholic Church”{{r|Pan}}. Both the second and third torii have been reinforced with new supporting columns as part of renovations by the church’s priest, Dai Hongji{{r|Pan}}. The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a [[baptismal font]]{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya; translated by Chen Xianruo |title=The Japanese Gods Who Crossed to Taiwan |publisher=Wild Man Culture |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=Pan Jidao |title=Changes in the Historical Site of the Shinjō Incident |journal=Taiwan Studies Bulletin |volume=82 |publisher=National Taiwan Library |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28–29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinjō Shrine}} [[Category:Shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Taiwan]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[ja:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E8%8A%B1%E8%93%AE%E6%B8%AF%E5%BA%81)]]

The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a [[baptismal font]]{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya; translated by Chen Xianruo |title=The Japanese Gods Who Crossed to Taiwan |publisher=Wild Man Culture |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=Pan Jidao |title=Changes in the Historical Site of the Shinjō Incident |journal=Taiwan Studies Bulletin |volume=82 |publisher=National Taiwan Library |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28–29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinjō Shrine}} [[Category:Shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Taiwan]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[ja:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E8%8A%B1%E8%93%AE%E6%B8%AF%E5%BA%81)]]

[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)-qq]] [[Category:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[ja:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE_(%E8%8A%B1%E8%93%AE%E6%B8%AF%E5%BA%81)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)-qq]] [[Category:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]][[zh:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Shrine |name=Shinjō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Shinjou_Tenshudou_xincheng_catholic_church_-_panoramio.jpg|250px]] |location=[[Hualien Port Prefecture]] Hualien District Kenkai Village Shinjō (current [[Hualien County]] [[Xincheng Township]] Boai Road 64) |deity=[[Prince Nogi]]
[[Ōkunitama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Sukuna-bikona-no-Mikoto]] |Rank=Shrine |consecration_year=[[1937]] (Shōwa 12) |Latitude degrees = 24 |Latitude minutes = 7 |Latitude seconds = 46.63 |N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 121 |Longitude minutes = 38 |Longitude seconds = 59.17 |E/W = E |ISO = TW |Map = Taiwan |Label position = left }} {{tw monument |name=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |formername= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |imagename= |othername= |formalname= |level=County Historic Site |class=[[Paifang]] |date=September 23, 2002 |time= |location={{flagicon|ROC}}[[Hualien County]] [[Xincheng Township]] Shinjō Village Boai Road 64 |website= |reg_url =https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 }} '''Shinjō Shrine''' (しんじょうしゃ) or '''Shinjō Jinja''' (しんじょうじんじゃ) was a shrine located in eastern Taiwan’s [[Hualien Port Prefecture]] {{ill|Hualien District|ja|花蓮郡}} {{ill|Kenkai Village|ja|研海莊}} during the Japanese colonial period. The shrine was built in the Shōwa era before World War II on the site of a commemorative stone “Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone” established in remembrance of Japanese soldiers who fell during conflicts with Taiwan’s indigenous peoples{{r|Bun}}{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, the shrine grounds were sold to the [[Catholic Church]], where the "Shinjō Catholic Church" was built. However, remnants such as the torii gates and foundations remain intact{{r|Bun}}. On February 23, 2005, the former shrine site was designated as a county historic site by the [[Hualien County]] authorities{{r|Bun}}. == History == At the beginning of the Japanese colonial era in [[1896]] (Meiji 29), a conflict known as the [[Shinjō Incident]] occurred in eastern Taiwan between the [[Imperial Japanese Army]] and the indigenous [[Taroko people]], resulting in the deaths of more than 10 Japanese soldiers. Memorial monuments such as the “Monument for the Fallen Soldiers” (established in 1920), the “Condolence Monument” (established in 1915), and the “Army Observation Post Fallen Commemoration Monument” (established in 1920) were built on the site{{r|Pan}}. Later, on October 25, 1937 (Shōwa 12), the "Shinjō Shrine" was constructed{{r|Kaneko}}{{r|Pan}}. At the shrine’s consecration ceremony, dignitaries such as the Governor of [[Hualien Port Prefecture]], Fujimura Kanta, and the Hualien District Governor, Yoshida Haruhisa, attended. Celebrations included youth sumo, dance performances, and kendo demonstrations{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, soldiers sent by the [[Kuomintang]] government destroyed the shrine buildings, reportedly causing several soldiers to suffer mental breakdowns. The site was subsequently left abandoned{{r|Pan}}. However, in 1956, Swiss priest Father Fu Guangye built a kindergarten on the western part of the shrine’s grounds, and in 1959, Swiss priest Father Sha Zhiyong constructed a clergy residence on the eastern side{{r|Pan}}. In 1964, the Ostine Catholic Church purchased the shrine grounds under the name {{ill|Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja|天主教花蓮教区}}, and a Catholic church was established{{r|Pan}}. The church was completed in 1966{{r|Pan}}. == Shrine Remains == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|The third torii]] Shinjō Shrine had three torii gates. The first torii stands near Shinjō Park along the pathway to the Catholic church and was converted into a [[paifang]] in 1979, with the words "Shinjō Park" embedded after cutting off the upper ends of the gate{{r|Pan}}. The second torii, located at the entrance to the church, bears the inscription “Catholic Church”{{r|Pan}}. Both the second and third torii have been reinforced with new supporting columns as part of renovations by the church’s priest, Dai Hongji{{r|Pan}}. The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a [[baptismal font]]{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya; translated by Chen Xianruo |title=The Japanese Gods Who Crossed to Taiwan |publisher=Wild Man Culture |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=Pan Jidao |title=Changes in the Historical Site of the Shinjō Incident |journal=Taiwan Studies Bulletin |volume=82 |publisher=National Taiwan Library |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28–29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinjō Shrine}} [[Category:Shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Taiwan]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:new pages]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:new pages]] [[ja:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E8%8A%B1%E8%93%AE%E6%B8%AF%E5%BA%81)]]

On February 23, 2005, the former shrine site was designated as a county historic site by the [[Hualien County]] authorities{{r|Bun}}. == History == At the beginning of the Japanese colonial era in [[1896]] (Meiji 29), a conflict known as the [[Shinjō Incident]] occurred in eastern Taiwan between the [[Imperial Japanese Army]] and the indigenous [[Taroko people]], resulting in the deaths of more than 10 Japanese soldiers. Memorial monuments such as the “Monument for the Fallen Soldiers” (established in 1920), the “Condolence Monument” (established in 1915), and the “Army Observation Post Fallen Commemoration Monument” (established in 1920) were built on the site{{r|Pan}}. Later, on October 25, 1937 (Shōwa 12), the "Shinjō Shrine" was constructed{{r|Kaneko}}{{r|Pan}}. At the shrine’s consecration ceremony, dignitaries such as the Governor of [[Hualien Port Prefecture]], Fujimura Kanta, and the Hualien District Governor, Yoshida Haruhisa, attended. Celebrations included youth sumo, dance performances, and kendo demonstrations{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, soldiers sent by the [[Kuomintang]] government destroyed the shrine buildings, reportedly causing several soldiers to suffer mental breakdowns. The site was subsequently left abandoned{{r|Pan}}. However, in 1956, Swiss priest Father Fu Guangye built a kindergarten on the western part of the shrine’s grounds, and in 1959, Swiss priest Father Sha Zhiyong constructed a clergy residence on the eastern side{{r|Pan}}. In 1964, the Ostine Catholic Church purchased the shrine grounds under the name {{ill|Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja|天主教花蓮教区}}, and a Catholic church was established{{r|Pan}}. The church was completed in 1966{{r|Pan}}. == Shrine Remains == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|The third torii]] Shinjō Shrine had three torii gates. The first torii stands near Shinjō Park along the pathway to the Catholic church and was converted into a [[paifang]] in 1979, with the words "Shinjō Park" embedded after cutting off the upper ends of the gate{{r|Pan}}. The second torii, located at the entrance to the church, bears the inscription “Catholic Church”{{r|Pan}}. Both the second and third torii have been reinforced with new supporting columns as part of renovations by the church’s priest, Dai Hongji{{r|Pan}}. The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a [[baptismal font]]{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya; [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)-qq]] [[Category:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[ja:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE_(%E8%8A%B1%E8%93%AE%E6%B8%AF%E5%BA%81)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)-qq]] [[Category:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]][[zh:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Shrine |name=Shinjō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Shinjou_Tenshudou_xincheng_catholic_church_-_panoramio.jpg|250px]] |location=[[Hualien Port Prefecture]] Hualien District Kenkai Village Shinjō (current [[Hualien County]] [[Xincheng Township]] Boai Road 64) |deity=[[Prince Nogi]]
[[Ōkunitama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Sukuna-bikona-no-Mikoto]] |Rank=Shrine |consecration_year=[[1937]] (Shōwa 12) |Latitude degrees = 24 |Latitude minutes = 7 |Latitude seconds = 46.63 |N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 121 |Longitude minutes = 38 |Longitude seconds = 59.17 |E/W = E |ISO = TW |Map = Taiwan |Label position = left }} {{tw monument |name=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |formername= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |imagename= |othername= |formalname= |level=County Historic Site |class=[[Paifang]] |date=September 23, 2002 |time= |location={{flagicon|ROC}}[[Hualien County]] [[Xincheng Township]] Shinjō Village Boai Road 64 |website= |reg_url =https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 }} '''Shinjō Shrine''' (しんじょうしゃ) or '''Shinjō Jinja''' (しんじょうじんじゃ) was a shrine located in eastern Taiwan’s [[Hualien Port Prefecture]] {{ill|Hualien District|ja|花蓮郡}} {{ill|Kenkai Village|ja|研海莊}} during the Japanese colonial period. The shrine was built in the Shōwa era before World War II on the site of a commemorative stone “Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone” established in remembrance of Japanese soldiers who fell during conflicts with Taiwan’s indigenous peoples{{r|Bun}}{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, the shrine grounds were sold to the [[Catholic Church]], where the "Shinjō Catholic Church" was built. However, remnants such as the torii gates and foundations remain intact{{r|Bun}}. On February 23, 2005, the former shrine site was designated as a county historic site by the [[Hualien County]] authorities{{r|Bun}}. == History == At the beginning of the Japanese colonial era in [[1896]] (Meiji 29), a conflict known as the [[Shinjō Incident]] occurred in eastern Taiwan between the [[Imperial Japanese Army]] and the indigenous [[Taroko people]], resulting in the deaths of more than 10 Japanese soldiers. Memorial monuments such as the “Monument for the Fallen Soldiers” (established in 1920), the “Condolence Monument” (established in 1915), and the “Army Observation Post Fallen Commemoration Monument” (established in 1920) were built on the site{{r|Pan}}. Later, on October 25, 1937 (Shōwa 12), the "Shinjō Shrine" was constructed{{r|Kaneko}}{{r|Pan}}. At the shrine’s consecration ceremony, dignitaries such as the Governor of [[Hualien Port Prefecture]], Fujimura Kanta, and the Hualien District Governor, Yoshida Haruhisa, attended. Celebrations included youth sumo, dance performances, and kendo demonstrations{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, soldiers sent by the [[Kuomintang]] government destroyed the shrine buildings, reportedly causing several soldiers to suffer mental breakdowns. The site was subsequently left abandoned{{r|Pan}}. However, in 1956, Swiss priest Father Fu Guangye built a kindergarten on the western part of the shrine’s grounds, and in 1959, Swiss priest Father Sha Zhiyong constructed a clergy residence on the eastern side{{r|Pan}}. In 1964, the Ostine Catholic Church purchased the shrine grounds under the name {{ill|Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja|天主教花蓮教区}}, and a Catholic church was established{{r|Pan}}. The church was completed in 1966{{r|Pan}}. == Shrine Remains == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|The third torii]] Shinjō Shrine had three torii gates. The first torii stands near Shinjō Park along the pathway to the Catholic church and was converted into a [[paifang]] in 1979, with the words "Shinjō Park" embedded after cutting off the upper ends of the gate{{r|Pan}}. The second torii, located at the entrance to the church, bears the inscription “Catholic Church”{{r|Pan}}. Both the second and third torii have been reinforced with new supporting columns as part of renovations by the church’s priest, Dai Hongji{{r|Pan}}. The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a [[baptismal font]]{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya; translated by Chen Xianruo |title=The Japanese Gods Who Crossed to Taiwan |publisher=Wild Man Culture |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=Pan Jidao |title=Changes in the Historical Site of the Shinjō Incident |journal=Taiwan Studies Bulletin |volume=82 |publisher=National Taiwan Library |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28–29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinjō Shrine}} [[Category:Shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Taiwan]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:new pages]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E8%8A%B1%E8%93%AE%E6%B8%AF%E5%BA%81)]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Removing this excess]]

[[Sukuna-bikona-no-Mikoto]] |Rank=Shrine |consecration_year=[[1937]] (Shōwa 12) |Latitude degrees = 24 |Latitude minutes = 7 |Latitude seconds = 46.63 |N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 121 |Longitude minutes = 38 |Longitude seconds = 59.17 |E/W = E |ISO = TW |Map = Taiwan |Label position = left }} {{tw monument |name=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |formername= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |imagename= |othername= |formalname= |level=County Historic Site |class=[[Paifang]] |date=September 23, 2002 |time= |location={{flagicon|ROC}}[[Hualien County]] [[Xincheng Township]] Shinjō Village Boai Road 64 |website= |reg_url =https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 }} '''Shinjō Shrine''' (しんじょうしゃ) or '''Shinjō Jinja''' (しんじょうじんじゃ) was a shrine located in eastern Taiwan’s [[Hualien Port Prefecture]] {{ill|Hualien District|ja|花蓮郡}} {{ill|Kenkai Village|ja|研海莊}} during the Japanese colonial period. The shrine was built in the Shōwa era before World War II on the site of a commemorative stone “Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone” established in remembrance of Japanese soldiers who fell during conflicts with Taiwan’s indigenous peoples{{r|Bun}}{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, the shrine grounds were sold to the [[Catholic Church]], where the "Shinjō Catholic Church" was built. However, remnants such as the torii gates and foundations remain intact{{r|Bun}}. On February 23, 2005, the former shrine site was designated as a county historic site by the [[Hualien County]] authorities{{r|Bun}}. == History == At the beginning of the Japanese colonial era in [[1896]] (Meiji 29), a conflict known as the [[Shinjō Incident]] occurred in eastern Taiwan between the [[Imperial Japanese Army]] and the indigenous [[Taroko people]], resulting in the deaths of more than 10 Japanese soldiers. Memorial monuments such as the “Monument for the Fallen Soldiers” (established in 1920), the “Condolence Monument” (established in 1915), and the “Army Observation Post Fallen Commemoration Monument” (established in 1920) were built on the site{{r|Pan}}. Later, on October 25, 1937 (Shōwa 12), the "Shinjō Shrine" was constructed{{r|Kaneko}}{{r|Pan}}. At the shrine’s consecration ceremony, dignitaries such as the Governor of [[Hualien Port Prefecture]], Fujimura Kanta, and the Hualien District Governor, Yoshida Haruhisa, attended. Celebrations included youth sumo, dance performances, and kendo demonstrations{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, soldiers sent by the [[Kuomintang]] government destroyed the shrine buildings, reportedly causing several soldiers to suffer mental breakdowns. The site was subsequently left abandoned{{r|Pan}}. However, in 1956, Swiss priest Father Fu Guangye built a kindergarten on the western part of the shrine’s grounds, and in 1959, Swiss priest Father Sha Zhiyong constructed a clergy residence on the eastern side{{r|Pan}}. In 1964, the Ostine Catholic Church purchased the shrine grounds under the name {{ill|Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja|天主教花蓮教区}}, and a Catholic church was established{{r|Pan}}. The church was completed in 1966{{r|Pan}}. == Shrine Remains == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|The third torii]] Shinjō Shrine had three torii gates. The first torii stands near Shinjō Park along the pathway to the Catholic church and was converted into a [[paifang]] in 1979, with the words "Shinjō Park" embedded after cutting off the upper ends of the gate{{r|Pan}}. The second torii, located at the entrance to the church, bears the inscription “Catholic Church”{{r|Pan}}. Both the second and third torii have been reinforced with new supporting columns as part of renovations by the church’s priest, Dai Hongji{{r|Pan}}. The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a [[baptismal font]]{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya; translated by Chen Xianruo |title=The Japanese Gods Who Crossed to Taiwan |publisher=Wild Man Culture |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=Pan Jidao |title=Changes in the Historical Site of the Shinjō Incident |journal=Taiwan Studies Bulletin |volume=82 |publisher=National Taiwan Library |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28–29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinjō Shrine}} [[Category:Shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Taiwan]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:new pages]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E8%8A%B1%E8%93%AE%E6%B8%AF%E5%BA%81)]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:tier 2]] [[Category:New]]

[[Category:Ugoodbye $1 categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)-qq]] [[Category:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[ja:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE_(%E8%8A%B1%E8%93%AE%E6%B8%AF%E5%BA%81)]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)-qq]] [[Category:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]][[zh:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Shrine |name=Shinjō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Shinjou_Tenshudou_xincheng_catholic_church_-_panoramio.jpg|250px]] |location=[[Hualien Port Prefecture]] Hualien District Kenkai Village Shinjō (current [[Hualien County]] [[Xincheng Township]] Boai Road 64) |deity=[[Prince Nogi]]
[[Ōkunitama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Sukuna-bikona-no-Mikoto]] |Rank=Shrine |consecration_year=[[1937]] (Shōwa 12) |Latitude degrees = 24 |Latitude minutes = 7 |Latitude seconds = 46.63 |N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 121 |Longitude minutes = 38 |Longitude seconds = 59.17 |E/W = E |ISO = TW |Map = Taiwan |Label position = left }} {{tw monument |name=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |formername= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |imagename= |othername= |formalname= |level=County Historic Site |class=[[Paifang]] |date=September 23, 2002 |time= |location={{flagicon|ROC}}[[Hualien County]] [[Xincheng Township]] Shinjō Village Boai Road 64 |website= |reg_url =https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 }} '''Shinjō Shrine''' (しんじょうしゃ) or '''Shinjō Jinja''' (しんじょうじんじゃ) was a shrine located in eastern Taiwan’s [[Hualien Port Prefecture]] {{ill|Hualien District|ja|花蓮郡}} {{ill|Kenkai Village|ja|研海莊}} during the Japanese colonial period. The shrine was built in the Shōwa era before World War II on the site of a commemorative stone “Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone” established in remembrance of Japanese soldiers who fell during conflicts with Taiwan’s indigenous peoples{{r|Bun}}{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, the shrine grounds were sold to the [[Catholic Church]], where the "Shinjō Catholic Church" was built. However, remnants such as the torii gates and foundations remain intact{{r|Bun}}. On February 23, 2005, the former shrine site was designated as a county historic site by the [[Hualien County]] authorities{{r|Bun}}. == History == At the beginning of the Japanese colonial era in [[1896]] (Meiji 29), a conflict known as the [[Shinjō Incident]] occurred in eastern Taiwan between the [[Imperial Japanese Army]] and the indigenous [[Taroko people]], resulting in the deaths of more than 10 Japanese soldiers. Memorial monuments such as the “Monument for the Fallen Soldiers” (established in 1920), the “Condolence Monument” (established in 1915), and the “Army Observation Post Fallen Commemoration Monument” (established in 1920) were built on the site{{r|Pan}}. Later, on October 25, 1937 (Shōwa 12), the "Shinjō Shrine" was constructed{{r|Kaneko}}{{r|Pan}}. At the shrine’s consecration ceremony, dignitaries such as the Governor of [[Hualien Port Prefecture]], Fujimura Kanta, and the Hualien District Governor, Yoshida Haruhisa, attended. Celebrations included youth sumo, dance performances, and kendo demonstrations{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, soldiers sent by the [[Kuomintang]] government destroyed the shrine buildings, reportedly causing several soldiers to suffer mental breakdowns. The site was subsequently left abandoned{{r|Pan}}. However, in 1956, Swiss priest Father Fu Guangye built a kindergarten on the western part of the shrine’s grounds, and in 1959, Swiss priest Father Sha Zhiyong constructed a clergy residence on the eastern side{{r|Pan}}. In 1964, the Ostine Catholic Church purchased the shrine grounds under the name {{ill|Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja|天主教花蓮教区}}, and a Catholic church was established{{r|Pan}}. The church was completed in 1966{{r|Pan}}. == Shrine Remains == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|The third torii]] Shinjō Shrine had three torii gates. The first torii stands near Shinjō Park along the pathway to the Catholic church and was converted into a [[paifang]] in 1979, with the words "Shinjō Park" embedded after cutting off the upper ends of the gate{{r|Pan}}. The second torii, located at the entrance to the church, bears the inscription “Catholic Church”{{r|Pan}}. Both the second and third torii have been reinforced with new supporting columns as part of renovations by the church’s priest, Dai Hongji{{r|Pan}}. The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a [[baptismal font]]{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya; translated by Chen Xianruo |title=The Japanese Gods Who Crossed to Taiwan |publisher=Wild Man Culture |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=Pan Jidao |title=Changes in the Historical Site of the Shinjō Incident |journal=Taiwan Studies Bulletin |volume=82 |publisher=National Taiwan Library |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28–29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinjō Shrine}} [[Category:Shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Taiwan]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:new pages]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E8%8A%B1%E8%93%AE%E6%B8%AF%E5%BA%81)]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:tier 2]] [[Category:New]]

[[Sukuna-bikona-no-Mikoto]] |Rank=Shrine |consecration_year=[[1937]] (Shōwa 12) |Latitude degrees = 24 |Latitude minutes = 7 |Latitude seconds = 46.63 |N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 121 |Longitude minutes = 38 |Longitude seconds = 59.17 |E/W = E |ISO = TW |Map = Taiwan |Label position = left }} {{tw monument |name=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |formername= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |imagename= |othername= |formalname= |level=County Historic Site |class=[[Paifang]] |date=September 23, 2002 |time= |location={{flagicon|ROC}}[[Hualien County]] [[Xincheng Township]] Shinjō Village Boai Road 64 |website= |reg_url =https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 }} '''Shinjō Shrine''' (しんじょうしゃ) or '''Shinjō Jinja''' (しんじょうじんじゃ) was a shrine located in eastern Taiwan’s [[Hualien Port Prefecture]] {{ill|Hualien District|ja|花蓮郡}} {{ill|Kenkai Village|ja|研海莊}} during the Japanese colonial period. The shrine was built in the Shōwa era before World War II on the site of a commemorative stone “Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone” established in remembrance of Japanese soldiers who fell during conflicts with Taiwan’s indigenous peoples{{r|Bun}}{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, the shrine grounds were sold to the [[Catholic Church]], where the "Shinjō Catholic Church" was built. However, remnants such as the torii gates and foundations remain intact{{r|Bun}}. On February 23, 2005, the former shrine site was designated as a county historic site by the [[Hualien County]] authorities{{r|Bun}}. == History == At the beginning of the Japanese colonial era in [[1896]] (Meiji 29), a conflict known as the [[Shinjō Incident]] occurred in eastern Taiwan between the [[Imperial Japanese Army]] and the indigenous [[Taroko people]], resulting in the deaths of more than 10 Japanese soldiers. Memorial monuments such as the “Monument for the Fallen Soldiers” (established in 1920), the “Condolence Monument” (established in 1915), and the “Army Observation Post Fallen Commemoration Monument” (established in 1920) were built on the site{{r|Pan}}. Later, on October 25, 1937 (Shōwa 12), the "Shinjō Shrine" was constructed{{r|Kaneko}}{{r|Pan}}. At the shrine’s consecration ceremony, dignitaries such as the Governor of [[Hualien Port Prefecture]], Fujimura Kanta, and the Hualien District Governor, Yoshida Haruhisa, attended. Celebrations included youth sumo, dance performances, and kendo demonstrations{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, soldiers sent by the [[Kuomintang]] government destroyed the shrine buildings, reportedly causing several soldiers to suffer mental breakdowns. The site was subsequently left abandoned{{r|Pan}}. However, in 1956, Swiss priest Father Fu Guangye built a kindergarten on the western part of the shrine’s grounds, and in 1959, Swiss priest Father Sha Zhiyong constructed a clergy residence on the eastern side{{r|Pan}}. In 1964, the Ostine Catholic Church purchased the shrine grounds under the name {{ill|Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja|天主教花蓮教区}}, and a Catholic church was established{{r|Pan}}. The church was completed in 1966{{r|Pan}}. == Shrine Remains == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|The third torii]] Shinjō Shrine had three torii gates. The first torii stands near Shinjō Park along the pathway to the Catholic church and was converted into a [[paifang]] in 1979, with the words "Shinjō Park" embedded after cutting off the upper ends of the gate{{r|Pan}}. The second torii, located at the entrance to the church, bears the inscription “Catholic Church”{{r|Pan}}. Both the second and third torii have been reinforced with new supporting columns as part of renovations by the church’s priest, Dai Hongji{{r|Pan}}. The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a [[baptismal font]]{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya; translated by Chen Xianruo |title=The Japanese Gods Who Crossed to Taiwan |publisher=Wild Man Culture |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=Pan Jidao |title=Changes in the Historical Site of the Shinjō Incident |journal=Taiwan Studies Bulletin |volume=82 |publisher=National Taiwan Library |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28–29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinjō Shrine}} [[Category:Shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Taiwan]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:new pages]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E8%8A%B1%E8%93%AE%E6%B8%AF%E5%BA%81)]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:hinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:tier 2]] [[Category:New]]

translated by Chen Xianruo |title=The Japanese Gods Who Crossed to Taiwan |publisher=Wild Man Culture |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=Pan Jidao |title=Changes in the Historical Site of the Shinjō Incident |journal=Taiwan Studies Bulletin |volume=82 |publisher=National Taiwan Library |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28–29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinjō Shrine}} [[Category:Shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Taiwan]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:new pages]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:new pages]] [[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E8%8A%B1%E8%93%AE%E6%B8%AF%E5%BA%81)]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:hinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:tier 2]] [[Category:New]]

revision.text:
[[Category:Ugoodbye $1 categories]] [[Category:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[ja:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE_(%E8%8A%B1%E8%93%AE%E6%B8%AF%E5%BA%81)]][[Category:Ugoodbye $1 categories]] [[Category:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]][[zh:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Shrine |name=Shinjō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Shinjou_Tenshudou_xincheng_catholic_church_-_panoramio.jpg|250px]] |location=[[Hualien Port Prefecture]] Hualien District Kenkai Village Shinjō (current [[Hualien County]] [[Xincheng Township]] Boai Road 64) |deity=[[Prince Nogi]]
[[Ōkunitama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Sukuna-bikona-no-Mikoto]] |Rank=Shrine |consecration_year=[[1937]] (Shōwa 12) |Latitude degrees = 24 |Latitude minutes = 7 |Latitude seconds = 46.63 |N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 121 |Longitude minutes = 38 |Longitude seconds = 59.17 |E/W = E |ISO = TW |Map = Taiwan |Label position = left }} {{tw monument |name=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |formername= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |imagename= |othername= |formalname= |level=County Historic Site |class=[[Paifang]] |date=September 23, 2002 |time= |location={{flagicon|ROC}}[[Hualien County]] [[Xincheng Township]] Shinjō Village Boai Road 64 |website= |reg_url =https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 }} '''Shinjō Shrine''' (しんじょうしゃ) or '''Shinjō Jinja''' (しんじょうじんじゃ) was a shrine located in eastern Taiwan’s [[Hualien Port Prefecture]] {{ill|Hualien District|ja|花蓮郡}} {{ill|Kenkai Village|ja|研海莊}} during the Japanese colonial period. The shrine was built in the Shōwa era before World War II on the site of a commemorative stone “Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone” established in remembrance of Japanese soldiers who fell during conflicts with Taiwan’s indigenous peoples{{r|Bun}}{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, the shrine grounds were sold to the [[Catholic Church]], where the "Shinjō Catholic Church" was built. However, remnants such as the torii gates and foundations remain intact{{r|Bun}}. On February 23, 2005, the former shrine site was designated as a county historic site by the [[Hualien County]] authorities{{r|Bun}}. == History == At the beginning of the Japanese colonial era in [[1896]] (Meiji 29), a conflict known as the [[Shinjō Incident]] occurred in eastern Taiwan between the [[Imperial Japanese Army]] and the indigenous [[Taroko people]], resulting in the deaths of more than 10 Japanese soldiers. Memorial monuments such as the “Monument for the Fallen Soldiers” (established in 1920), the “Condolence Monument” (established in 1915), and the “Army Observation Post Fallen Commemoration Monument” (established in 1920) were built on the site{{r|Pan}}. Later, on October 25, 1937 (Shōwa 12), the "Shinjō Shrine" was constructed{{r|Kaneko}}{{r|Pan}}. At the shrine’s consecration ceremony, dignitaries such as the Governor of [[Hualien Port Prefecture]], Fujimura Kanta, and the Hualien District Governor, Yoshida Haruhisa, attended. Celebrations included youth sumo, dance performances, and kendo demonstrations{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, soldiers sent by the [[Kuomintang]] government destroyed the shrine buildings, reportedly causing several soldiers to suffer mental breakdowns. The site was subsequently left abandoned{{r|Pan}}. However, in 1956, Swiss priest Father Fu Guangye built a kindergarten on the western part of the shrine’s grounds, and in 1959, Swiss priest Father Sha Zhiyong constructed a clergy residence on the eastern side{{r|Pan}}. In 1964, the Ostine Catholic Church purchased the shrine grounds under the name {{ill|Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja|天主教花蓮教区}}, and a Catholic church was established{{r|Pan}}. The church was completed in 1966{{r|Pan}}. == Shrine Remains == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|The third torii]] Shinjō Shrine had three torii gates. The first torii stands near Shinjō Park along the pathway to the Catholic church and was converted into a [[paifang]] in 1979, with the words "Shinjō Park" embedded after cutting off the upper ends of the gate{{r|Pan}}. The second torii, located at the entrance to the church, bears the inscription “Catholic Church”{{r|Pan}}. Both the second and third torii have been reinforced with new supporting columns as part of renovations by the church’s priest, Dai Hongji{{r|Pan}}. The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a [[baptismal font]]{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya; translated by Chen Xianruo |title=The Japanese Gods Who Crossed to Taiwan |publisher=Wild Man Culture |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=Pan Jidao |title=Changes in the Historical Site of the Shinjō Incident |journal=Taiwan Studies Bulletin |volume=82 |publisher=National Taiwan Library |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28–29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinjō Shrine}} [[Category:Shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Defunct buildings and structures in Taiwan]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:new pages]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:new pages]] [[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E8%8A%B1%E8%93%AE%E6%B8%AF%E5%BA%81)]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:hinjō Shrine (Taiwan)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:tier 2]] [[Category:New]]

The shrine was built in the Shōwa era before World War II on the site of a commemorative stone “Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone” established in remembrance of Japanese soldiers who fell during conflicts with Taiwan’s indigenous peoples{{r|Bun}}{{r|Kaneko}}. Memorial monuments such as the “Monument for the Fallen Soldiers” (established in 1920), the “Condolence Monument” (established in 1915), and the “Army Observation Post Fallen Commemoration Monument” (established in 1920) were built on the site{{r|Pan}}. At the shrine’s consecration ceremony, dignitaries such as the Governor of [[Hualien Port Prefecture]], Fujimura Kanta, and the Hualien District Governor, Yoshida Haruhisa, attended. The site was subsequently left abandoned{{r|Pan}}. However, in 1956, Swiss priest Father Fu Guangye built a kindergarten on the western part of the shrine’s grounds, and in 1959, Swiss priest Father Sha Zhiyong constructed a clergy residence on the eastern side{{r|Pan}}. In 1964, the Ostine Catholic Church purchased the shrine grounds under the name {{ill|Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja|天主教花蓮教区}}, and a Catholic church was established{{r|Pan}}. The church was completed in 1966{{r|Pan}}. == Shrine Remains == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|The third torii]] Shinjō Shrine had three torii gates. [[Sukuna-bikona-no-Mikoto]] |Rank=Shrine |consecration_year=[[1937]] (Shōwa 12) |Latitude degrees = 24 |Latitude minutes = 7 |Latitude seconds = 46.63 |N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 121 |Longitude minutes = 38 |Longitude seconds = 59.17 |E/W = E |ISO = TW |Map = Taiwan |Label position = left }} {{tw monument |name=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |formername= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |imagename= |othername= |formalname= |level=County Historic Site |class=[[Paifang]] |date=September 23, 2002 |time= |location={{flagicon|ROC}}[[Hualien County]] [[Xincheng Township]] Shinjō Village Boai Road 64 |website= |reg_url =https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 }} '''Shinjō Shrine''' (しんじょうしゃ) or '''Shinjō Jinja''' (しんじょうじんじゃ) was a shrine located in eastern Taiwan’s [[Hualien Port Prefecture]] {{ill|Hualien District|ja|花蓮郡}} {{ill|Kenkai Village|ja|研海莊}} during the Japanese colonial period. The shrine was built in the Shōwa era before World War II on the site of a commemorative stone “Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone” established in remembrance of Japanese soldiers who fell during conflicts with Taiwan’s indigenous peoples{{r|Bun}}{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, the shrine grounds were sold to the [[Catholic Church]], where the "Shinjō Catholic Church" was built. However, remnants such as the torii gates and foundations remain intact{{r|Bun}}. On February 23, 2005, the former shrine site was designated as a county historic site by the [[Hualien County]] authorities{{r|Bun}}. == History == At the beginning of the Japanese colonial era in [[1896]] (Meiji 29), a conflict known as the [[Shinjō Incident]] occurred in eastern Taiwan between the [[Imperial Japanese Army]] and the indigenous [[Taroko people]], resulting in the deaths of more than 10 Japanese soldiers. Memorial monuments such as the “Monument for the Fallen Soldiers” (established in 1920), the “Condolence Monument” (established in 1915), and the “Army Observation Post Fallen Commemoration Monument” (established in 1920) were built on the site{{r|Pan}}. Later, on October 25, 1937 (Shōwa 12), the "Shinjō Shrine" was constructed{{r|Kaneko}}{{r|Pan}}. At the shrine’s consecration ceremony, dignitaries such as the Governor of [[Hualien Port Prefecture]], Fujimura Kanta, and the Hualien District Governor, Yoshida Haruhisa, attended. Celebrations included youth sumo, dance performances, and kendo demonstrations{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, soldiers sent by the [[Kuomintang]] government destroyed the shrine buildings, reportedly causing several soldiers to suffer mental breakdowns. The site was subsequently left abandoned{{r|Pan}}. However, in 1956, Swiss priest Father Fu Guangye built a kindergarten on the western part of the shrine’s grounds, and in 1959, Swiss priest Father Sha Zhiyong constructed a clergy residence on the eastern side{{r|Pan}}. In 1964, the Ostine Catholic Church purchased the shrine grounds under the name {{ill|Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja|天主教花蓮教区}}, and a Catholic church was established{{r|Pan}}. The church was completed in 1966{{r|Pan}}. == Shrine Remains == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|The third torii]] Shinjō Shrine had three torii gates. The first torii stands near Shinjō Park along the pathway to the Catholic church and was converted into a [[paifang]] in 1979, with the words "Shinjō Park" embedded after cutting off the upper ends of the gate{{r|Pan}}. The second torii, located at the entrance to the church, bears the inscription “Catholic Church”{{r|Pan}}. Both the second and third torii have been reinforced with new supporting columns as part of renovations by the church’s priest, Dai Hongji{{r|Pan}}. The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a [[baptismal font]]{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya;
