[[co:commons:Category:Shinjō Shrine]][[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:新城社]] [[co:commons:Category:Shinjō Shrine]][[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:新城社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Shinjō Shrine]][[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:新城社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q104837484]] Wikidata name: Shinjō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:27, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Shinjō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:新城神社舊址08.jpg |location=[[Hualien Port Prefecture]] Hualien District Kenkai Village Shinjō (current {{ill|Hualien County|en|Hualien County|WD=Q249868|lt=Hualien County}} {{ill|Xincheng Township|en|Xincheng Township|WD=Q3860595|lt=Xincheng Township}} Boai Road 64) |deity=[[Prince Nogi]]
[[Ōkunitama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Sukuna-bikona-no-Mikoto]] |Rank=Shrine |consecration_year={{ill|1937|en|1937|WD=Q18647|lt=1937}} (Shōwa 12) |Latitude degrees = 24 |Latitude minutes = 7 |Latitude seconds = 46.63 |N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 121 |Longitude minutes = 38 |Longitude seconds = 59.17 |E/W = E |ISO = TW |Map = Taiwan |Label position = left |location = {{ill|Xincheng Township|WD=Q712969}} |coordinates = {{Coord|24.12962|N|121.64977|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{tw monument |name=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |formername= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |imagename= |othername= |formalname= |level=County Historic Site |class={{ill|Paifang|en|Paifang|WD=Q681188|lt=Paifang}} |date=September 23, 2002 |time= |location={{flagicon|ROC}}{{ill|Hualien County|en|Hualien County|WD=Q249868|lt=Hualien County}} {{ill|Xincheng Township|en|Xincheng Township|WD=Q3860595|lt=Xincheng Township}} Shinjō Village Boai Road 64 |website= |reg_url =https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 }} '''Shinjō Shrine''' (しんじょうしゃ) or '''Shinjō Jinja''' (しんじょうじんじゃ) was a shrine located in eastern Taiwan’s [[Hualien Port Prefecture]] {{ill|Hualien District|ja|花蓮郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hualien District|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|Kenkai Village|ja|研海莊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenkai Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} during the Japanese colonial period. The shrine was built in the Shōwa era before World War II on the site of a commemorative stone “Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone” established in remembrance of Japanese soldiers who fell during conflicts with Taiwan’s indigenous peoples{{r|Bun}}{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, the shrine grounds were sold to the {{ill|Catholic Church|en|Catholic Church|WD=Q9592|lt=Catholic Church}}, where the "Shinjō Catholic Church" was built. However, remnants such as the torii gates and foundations remain intact{{r|Bun}}. On February 23, 2005, the former shrine site was designated as a county historic site by the {{ill|Hualien County|en|Hualien County|WD=Q249868|lt=Hualien County}} authorities{{r|Bun}}. == History == At the beginning of the Japanese colonial era in {{ill|1896|en|1896|WD=Q7846|lt=1896}} (Meiji 29), a conflict known as the [[Shinjō Incident]] occurred in eastern Taiwan between the {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|en|Imperial Japanese Army|WD=Q276535|lt=Imperial Japanese Army}} and the indigenous {{ill|Taroko people|en|Taroko people|WD=Q709963|lt=Taroko people}}, resulting in the deaths of more than 10 Japanese soldiers. Memorial monuments such as the “Monument for the Fallen Soldiers” (established in 1920), the “Condolence Monument” (established in 1915), and the “Army Observation Post Fallen Commemoration Monument” (established in 1920) were built on the site{{r|Pan}}. Later, on October 25, 1937 (Shōwa 12), the "Shinjō Shrine" was constructed{{r|Kaneko}}{{r|Pan}}. At the shrine’s consecration ceremony, dignitaries such as the Governor of [[Hualien Port Prefecture]], Fujimura Kanta, and the Hualien District Governor, Yoshida Haruhisa, attended. Celebrations included youth sumo, dance performances, and kendo demonstrations{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, soldiers sent by the {{ill|Kuomintang|en|Kuomintang|WD=Q31113|lt=Kuomintang}} government destroyed the shrine buildings, reportedly causing several soldiers to suffer mental breakdowns. The site was subsequently left abandoned{{r|Pan}}. However, in 1956, Swiss priest Father Fu Guangye built a kindergarten on the western part of the shrine’s grounds, and in 1959, Swiss priest Father Sha Zhiyong constructed a clergy residence on the eastern side{{r|Pan}}. In 1964, the Ostine Catholic Church purchased the shrine grounds under the name {{ill|Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja|天主教花蓮教区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and a Catholic church was established{{r|Pan}}. The church was completed in 1966{{r|Pan}}. == Shrine Remains == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|The third torii]] Shinjō Shrine had three torii gates. The first torii stands near Shinjō Park along the pathway to the Catholic church and was converted into a {{ill|Paifang|en|Paifang|WD=Q681188|lt=Paifang}} in 1979, with the words "Shinjō Park" embedded after cutting off the upper ends of the gate{{r|Pan}}. The second torii, located at the entrance to the church, bears the inscription “Catholic Church”{{r|Pan}}. Both the second and third torii have been reinforced with new supporting columns as part of renovations by the church’s priest, Dai Hongji{{r|Pan}}. The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a {{ill|baptismal font|en|baptismal font|WD=Q208820|lt=baptismal font}}{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya; translated by Chen Xianruo |title=The Japanese Gods Who Crossed to Taiwan |publisher=Wild Man Culture |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=Pan Jidao |title=Changes in the Historical Site of the Shinjō Incident |journal=Taiwan Studies Bulletin |volume=82 |publisher=National Taiwan Library |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28–29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{translated page|ja|新城神社 (花蓮港庁)|version=98673886|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[zh:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Commons category|Shinjō Shrine}} [[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[zh:新城社]] {{Commons category|Shinjō Shrine}} [[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[zh:新城社]] {{wikidata link|Q104837484}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Former buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a {{ill|baptismal font|en|baptismal font|WD=Q208820|lt=baptismal font}}{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya; translated by Chen Xianruo |title=The Japanese Gods Who Crossed to Taiwan |publisher=Wild Man Culture |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=Pan Jidao |title=Changes in the Historical Site of the Shinjō Incident |journal=Taiwan Studies Bulletin |volume=82 |publisher=National Taiwan Library |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28–29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{translated page|ja|新城神社 (花蓮港庁)|version=98673886|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[zh:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Commons category|Shinjō Shrine}} [[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[zh:新城社]] {{Commons category|Shinjō Shrine}} [[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[zh:新城社]] {{wikidata link|Q104837484}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Former buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Shinjō Shrine]][[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:新城社]] [[co:commons:Category:Shinjō Shrine]][[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:新城社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Shinjō Shrine]][[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:新城社]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Shinjō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:新城神社舊址08.jpg |location=[[Hualien Port Prefecture]] Hualien District Kenkai Village Shinjō (current {{ill|Hualien County|en|Hualien County|WD=Q249868|lt=Hualien County}} {{ill|Xincheng Township|en|Xincheng Township|WD=Q3860595|lt=Xincheng Township}} Boai Road 64) |deity=[[Prince Nogi]]
[[Ōkunitama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Sukuna-bikona-no-Mikoto]] |Rank=Shrine |consecration_year={{ill|1937|en|1937|WD=Q18647|lt=1937}} (Shōwa 12) |Latitude degrees = 24 |Latitude minutes = 7 |Latitude seconds = 46.63 |N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 121 |Longitude minutes = 38 |Longitude seconds = 59.17 |E/W = E |ISO = TW |Map = Taiwan |Label position = left |location = {{ill|Xincheng Township|WD=Q712969}} |coordinates = {{Coord|24.12962|N|121.64977|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{tw monument |name=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |formername= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |imagename= |othername= |formalname= |level=County Historic Site |class={{ill|Paifang|en|Paifang|WD=Q681188|lt=Paifang}} |date=September 23, 2002 |time= |location={{flagicon|ROC}}{{ill|Hualien County|en|Hualien County|WD=Q249868|lt=Hualien County}} {{ill|Xincheng Township|en|Xincheng Township|WD=Q3860595|lt=Xincheng Township}} Shinjō Village Boai Road 64 |website= |reg_url =https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 }} '''Shinjō Shrine''' (しんじょうしゃ) or '''Shinjō Jinja''' (しんじょうじんじゃ) was a shrine located in eastern Taiwan’s [[Hualien Port Prefecture]] {{ill|Hualien District|ja|花蓮郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hualien District|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|Kenkai Village|ja|研海莊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenkai Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} during the Japanese colonial period. The shrine was built in the Shōwa era before World War II on the site of a commemorative stone “Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone” established in remembrance of Japanese soldiers who fell during conflicts with Taiwan’s indigenous peoples{{r|Bun}}{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, the shrine grounds were sold to the {{ill|Catholic Church|en|Catholic Church|WD=Q9592|lt=Catholic Church}}, where the "Shinjō Catholic Church" was built. However, remnants such as the torii gates and foundations remain intact{{r|Bun}}. On February 23, 2005, the former shrine site was designated as a county historic site by the {{ill|Hualien County|en|Hualien County|WD=Q249868|lt=Hualien County}} authorities{{r|Bun}}. == History == At the beginning of the Japanese colonial era in {{ill|1896|en|1896|WD=Q7846|lt=1896}} (Meiji 29), a conflict known as the [[Shinjō Incident]] occurred in eastern Taiwan between the {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|en|Imperial Japanese Army|WD=Q276535|lt=Imperial Japanese Army}} and the indigenous {{ill|Taroko people|en|Taroko people|WD=Q709963|lt=Taroko people}}, resulting in the deaths of more than 10 Japanese soldiers. Memorial monuments such as the “Monument for the Fallen Soldiers” (established in 1920), the “Condolence Monument” (established in 1915), and the “Army Observation Post Fallen Commemoration Monument” (established in 1920) were built on the site{{r|Pan}}. Later, on October 25, 1937 (Shōwa 12), the "Shinjō Shrine" was constructed{{r|Kaneko}}{{r|Pan}}. At the shrine’s consecration ceremony, dignitaries such as the Governor of [[Hualien Port Prefecture]], Fujimura Kanta, and the Hualien District Governor, Yoshida Haruhisa, attended. Celebrations included youth sumo, dance performances, and kendo demonstrations{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, soldiers sent by the {{ill|Kuomintang|en|Kuomintang|WD=Q31113|lt=Kuomintang}} government destroyed the shrine buildings, reportedly causing several soldiers to suffer mental breakdowns. The site was subsequently left abandoned{{r|Pan}}. However, in 1956, Swiss priest Father Fu Guangye built a kindergarten on the western part of the shrine’s grounds, and in 1959, Swiss priest Father Sha Zhiyong constructed a clergy residence on the eastern side{{r|Pan}}. In 1964, the Ostine Catholic Church purchased the shrine grounds under the name {{ill|Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja|天主教花蓮教区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and a Catholic church was established{{r|Pan}}. The church was completed in 1966{{r|Pan}}. == Shrine Remains == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|The third torii]] Shinjō Shrine had three torii gates. The first torii stands near Shinjō Park along the pathway to the Catholic church and was converted into a {{ill|Paifang|en|Paifang|WD=Q681188|lt=Paifang}} in 1979, with the words "Shinjō Park" embedded after cutting off the upper ends of the gate{{r|Pan}}. The second torii, located at the entrance to the church, bears the inscription “Catholic Church”{{r|Pan}}. Both the second and third torii have been reinforced with new supporting columns as part of renovations by the church’s priest, Dai Hongji{{r|Pan}}. The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a {{ill|baptismal font|en|baptismal font|WD=Q208820|lt=baptismal font}}{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya; translated by Chen Xianruo |title=The Japanese Gods Who Crossed to Taiwan |publisher=Wild Man Culture |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=Pan Jidao |title=Changes in the Historical Site of the Shinjō Incident |journal=Taiwan Studies Bulletin |volume=82 |publisher=National Taiwan Library |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28–29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{translated page|ja|新城神社 (花蓮港庁)|version=98673886|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[zh:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Commons category|Shinjō Shrine}} [[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[zh:新城社]] {{Commons category|Shinjō Shrine}} [[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[zh:新城社]] {{wikidata link|Q104837484}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Former buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

translated by Chen Xianruo |title=The Japanese Gods Who Crossed to Taiwan |publisher=Wild Man Culture |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=Pan Jidao |title=Changes in the Historical Site of the Shinjō Incident |journal=Taiwan Studies Bulletin |volume=82 |publisher=National Taiwan Library |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28–29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{translated page|ja|新城神社 (花蓮港庁)|version=98673886|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[zh:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Commons category|Shinjō Shrine}} [[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[zh:新城社]] {{Commons category|Shinjō Shrine}} [[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[zh:新城社]] {{wikidata link|Q104837484}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Former buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

[[co:commons:Category:Shinjō Shrine]][[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:新城社]] [[co:commons:Category:Shinjō Shrine]][[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:新城社]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Shinjō Shrine]][[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:新城社]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Shinjō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:新城神社舊址08.jpg |location=[[Hualien Port Prefecture]] Hualien District Kenkai Village Shinjō (current {{ill|Hualien County|en|Hualien County|WD=Q249868|lt=Hualien County|1=Hualien County}} {{ill|Xincheng Township|en|Xincheng Township|WD=Q3860595|lt=Xincheng Township|1=Xincheng Township}} Boai Road 64) |deity=[[Prince Nogi]]
[[Ōkunitama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto]]
[[Sukuna-bikona-no-Mikoto]] |Rank=Shrine |consecration_year={{ill|1937|en|1937|WD=Q18647|lt=1937|1=1937}} (Shōwa 12) |Latitude degrees = 24 |Latitude minutes = 7 |Latitude seconds = 46.63 |N/S = N |Longitude degrees = 121 |Longitude minutes = 38 |Longitude seconds = 59.17 |E/W = E |ISO = TW |Map = Taiwan |Label position = left |location = {{ill|Xincheng Township|WD=Q712969|1=Xincheng, Hualien}} |coordinates = {{Coord|24.12962|N|121.64977|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{tw monument |name=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |formername= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |imagename= |othername= |formalname= |level=County Historic Site |class={{ill|Paifang|en|Paifang|WD=Q681188|lt=Paifang|1=Paifang}} |date=September 23, 2002 |time= |location={{flagicon|ROC}}{{ill|Hualien County|en|Hualien County|WD=Q249868|lt=Hualien County|1=Hualien County}} {{ill|Xincheng Township|en|Xincheng Township|WD=Q3860595|lt=Xincheng Township|1=Xincheng Township}} Shinjō Village Boai Road 64 |website= |reg_url =https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 }} '''Shinjō Shrine''' (しんじょうしゃ) or '''Shinjō Jinja''' (しんじょうじんじゃ) was a shrine located in eastern Taiwan’s [[Hualien Port Prefecture]] {{ill|Hualien District|ja|花蓮郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hualien District|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|Kenkai Village|ja|研海莊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenkai Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} during the Japanese colonial period. The shrine was built in the Shōwa era before World War II on the site of a commemorative stone “Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone” established in remembrance of Japanese soldiers who fell during conflicts with Taiwan’s indigenous peoples{{r|Bun}}{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, the shrine grounds were sold to the {{ill|Catholic Church|en|Catholic Church|WD=Q9592|lt=Catholic Church|1=Catholic Church}}, where the "Shinjō Catholic Church" was built. However, remnants such as the torii gates and foundations remain intact{{r|Bun}}. On February 23, 2005, the former shrine site was designated as a county historic site by the {{ill|Hualien County|en|Hualien County|WD=Q249868|lt=Hualien County|1=Hualien County}} authorities{{r|Bun}}. == History == At the beginning of the Japanese colonial era in {{ill|1896|en|1896|WD=Q7846|lt=1896|1=1896}} (Meiji 29), a conflict known as the [[Shinjō Incident]] occurred in eastern Taiwan between the {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|en|Imperial Japanese Army|WD=Q276535|lt=Imperial Japanese Army|1=Imperial Japanese Army}} and the indigenous {{ill|Taroko people|en|Taroko people|WD=Q709963|lt=Taroko people|1=Taroko people}}, resulting in the deaths of more than 10 Japanese soldiers. Memorial monuments such as the “Monument for the Fallen Soldiers” (established in 1920), the “Condolence Monument” (established in 1915), and the “Army Observation Post Fallen Commemoration Monument” (established in 1920) were built on the site{{r|Pan}}. Later, on October 25, 1937 (Shōwa 12), the "Shinjō Shrine" was constructed{{r|Kaneko}}{{r|Pan}}. At the shrine’s consecration ceremony, dignitaries such as the Governor of [[Hualien Port Prefecture]], Fujimura Kanta, and the Hualien District Governor, Yoshida Haruhisa, attended. Celebrations included youth sumo, dance performances, and kendo demonstrations{{r|Kaneko}}. After World War II, soldiers sent by the {{ill|Kuomintang|en|Kuomintang|WD=Q31113|lt=Kuomintang|1=Kuomintang}} government destroyed the shrine buildings, reportedly causing several soldiers to suffer mental breakdowns. The site was subsequently left abandoned{{r|Pan}}. However, in 1956, Swiss priest Father Fu Guangye built a kindergarten on the western part of the shrine’s grounds, and in 1959, Swiss priest Father Sha Zhiyong constructed a clergy residence on the eastern side{{r|Pan}}. In 1964, the Ostine Catholic Church purchased the shrine grounds under the name {{ill|Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja|天主教花蓮教区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Catholic Diocese of Hualien|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and a Catholic church was established{{r|Pan}}. The church was completed in 1966{{r|Pan}}. == Shrine Remains == [[File:Former Site of Xincheng Shinto Shrine, Xincheng Township, Hualien County (Taiwan).jpg|250px|thumb|The third torii]] Shinjō Shrine had three torii gates. The first torii stands near Shinjō Park along the pathway to the Catholic church and was converted into a {{ill|Paifang|en|Paifang|WD=Q681188|lt=Paifang|1=Paifang}} in 1979, with the words "Shinjō Park" embedded after cutting off the upper ends of the gate{{r|Pan}}. The second torii, located at the entrance to the church, bears the inscription “Catholic Church”{{r|Pan}}. Both the second and third torii have been reinforced with new supporting columns as part of renovations by the church’s priest, Dai Hongji{{r|Pan}}. The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a {{ill|baptismal font|en|baptismal font|WD=Q208820|lt=baptismal font|1=Baptismal font}}{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya; translated by Chen Xianruo |title=The Japanese Gods Who Crossed to Taiwan |publisher=Wild Man Culture |date=2020-07-08 |isbn=978-986-384-437-2 |pages=320-322 }} {{cite journal |url=https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |author=Pan Jidao |title=Changes in the Historical Site of the Shinjō Incident |journal=Taiwan Studies Bulletin |volume=82 |publisher=National Taiwan Library |issn=1999-1851 |pages=28–29 |date=2014-07-10 |access-date=2021-01-16 |archive-date=2021-01-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210123141123/https://www.ntl.edu.tw/public/Attachment/4711957159.pdf |dead-url=no }} }} {{translated page|ja|新城神社 (花蓮港庁)|version=98673886|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[zh:%E6%96%B0%E5%9F%8E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Commons category|Shinjō Shrine}} [[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[zh:新城社]] {{Commons category|Shinjō Shrine}} [[ja:新城神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[zh:新城社]] {{wikidata link|Q104837484}} [[Category:Former buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

The second torii, located at the entrance to the church, bears the inscription “Catholic Church”{{r|Pan}}. Both the second and third torii have been reinforced with new supporting columns as part of renovations by the church’s priest, Dai Hongji{{r|Pan}}. The hand-washing basin was relocated inside the church and repurposed as a {{ill|baptismal font|en|baptismal font|WD=Q208820|lt=baptismal font|1=Baptismal font}}{{r|Pan}}. == Gallery == File:Shinjō Shrine Site.jpg|Statue of the Virgin Mary erected on the shrine’s main foundation File:Monument for Fallen Soldiers of Shinjō Incident.jpg|"Monument for Fallen Soldiers" (Shinjō Incident Memorial Stone) File:First Torii of Shinjō Shrine.jpg|The first torii == See Also == * [[Shinjō Incident]] * [[Indigenous Policies]] == References == {{commonscat|Shinjō Shrine}} {{reflist|refs= {{cite web |url=https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |title=Former Site of Shinjō Shrine |accessdate=2021-01-16 |publisher=Ministry of Culture |work=National Cultural Heritage Database |archive-date=2021-01-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210121224606/https://nchdb.boch.gov.tw/assets/overview/monument/20050223000002 |dead-url=no }} {{cite book|author=Kaneko Nobuya;
